item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image is from here, whose images are for sale only. As such, you need to buy a license to use their images, it is not compliant CC 3.0, and thus has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The effect forces a sufficiently unlikely death, and actively prevents anything else from happening.
So what if you take that automatic shock collar and pointed the trigger at some other "pseudorandom" phenomenon? Then you could force it to make things happen, as long as they were fittingly unlikely.

The device could be linked to a computer that scans headlines for the phrase "Wishun wins $10 Million lottery" or "Lab accident reveals cure for cancer" or "<Insert public figure inconvenient to Foundation interests> killed by lightning".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So it has to decompose for the life cycle to reset, right?

Why couldn't it simply be sterilized and frozen, instead of affecting the weather.

item.title: Something Strange.
item.content:encoded:
I've found somewhere maybe not rigorous.
I think that "4 x 4 x 5 meters" should be "4m x 4m x 5m" or "4 x 4 x 5 m^3".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know about you, but I found that sentence to be perfectly understandable. I do agree with everything else you said, though. If someone could make testing logs for this skip, that would be awesome. I would, but me and writing don't really mix.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
During the second stage of its cycle SCP-1436 manipulates probability to cause its own death.

We have to get through an awful lot of verbiage to get to this sentence, which encapsulates all of the more interesting of the two anomalies - the other being the phoenix lifecycle.

A bird that interferes with causality as part of it's breeding cycle - fine. I don't think we need the "born from its own ashes" aspect at all.

As others have said, cut back on the description, and add some test logs for monstrously improbable events - pencils balancing on their tips, bombs failing in bizarre ways at just the right moment, Foundation personnel being reunited with college sweethearts by ruptures in the boundaries between universes. Maybe even a researcher suggesting ways to weaponise the bird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edit: Nevermind anything I said here. Move on now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll concur with everyone else here: the tone is spot-on, and there's a lot of potential (for which I upvote) but it really needs more. It's crying out for a log of amusing improbable deaths.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea I had was that the air filters would be slightly different every day (just via random variation) but the weather thing is way cooler/scarier. BTW you offered to look the article over from a biology perspective, is anything amiss in that regard?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry I'm late, but would you still like me to look this over?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But if the bird affects probability to get itself killed, the easiest way for it to do so will be to affect the random-number generator linked to its termination collar… and the clearest way to do that is to affect the atmospheric conditions from which the generator draws its randomness.

Thus, unless the generator's drawing from atmospheric conditions inside the aviary (which wouldn't be very random at all), the bird is probably affecting the weather.

Which is horribly awesome, and exactly the kind of fridge logic I love to pieces.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Im willing to lend a hand (review+critique along with idea brainstorming) if you need it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I addressed the egg issue, but I don't see where I should be using commas in my containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, there I will add a research log, but life's been very busy for me lately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, not really as the aviary is climate controlled, and therefore any bird induced changes to the air will therefore be limited to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy shit, that brings an entirely new depth to this. It's like the butterfly effect, but with a suicidal bird instead of an insect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
based on a pseudo-random number generator dependent on atmospheric conditions

The fridge logic just hit me here that the foundation is inadvertently affecting the weather by doing this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the entry. Personally, I've always had a liking for the entries that are not "OMG, suddenly kills you!" I like weirdness. This one I like because of the weird causes of death. However, I would really like to see a research/observation log.

Minor nitpick. You use the word "corpse". Technically, "corpse" is a term for a dead human. The proper term for a dead animal would be carcass. Other than that, I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the idea, and see a lot of potential for it. The lack of commas got distracting (check you containment procedures), but was alright overall. Addenda and incident logs are definitely in order, though. Maybe even a capture log? Also, maybe specify where the egg reappears? Just saying it does says nothing. Perhaps the body dies, forms the egg, then rapidly decomposes around said egg? Just a quick idea.

Gave an upvote to keep it going, but may revoke if not improved upon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. Maybe have an incident log regarding its being taken into custody. Or an interview with a member of the MTF involved. A bird like that in a helicopter, or in a car on the freeway for that matter, is sure to cause some chaos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two more nitpicks:

1) Where does the egg reappear? I assume that it's where the old body finished decomposing, but it probably wouldn't hurt to specify here.

2) How do we know that it's SCP-1436 doing the probability manipulation? How do we know it's not the space alien ghosts on a station in mars (in a different universe, where the moon really IS made of cheese)? Saying SCP-1436 is doing it is at best speculation from the researchers.

During the second stage of its cycle SCP-1436 manipulates probability to cause its own death.

Would be better as (In my opinion)

During the second stage of its cycle, probability appears to be manipulated in order to lead up to situations leading to SCP-1436's death.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Welp, there goes any desire to go to Japan.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I think it could be improved by an incident log, and possibly with stricken/addended containment procedures but overall I like it anyway. I especially like the unique containment method:

"The current activation procedure requires that SCP-1436 is fitted with a device programmed to discharge an 8000 volt pulse, sufficient to kill SCP-1436, based on a pseudo-random number generator dependent on atmospheric conditions. The device is to be reattached three weeks into every cycle. The chance of these conditions being met and the pulse being discharged outside of a SCP-1436 event is approximately 1 in 10,000 per day. The device must be confirmed to be functioning when reattached and examined again every day of during the second stage of SCP-1436’s cycle."

You should make one incident where the volt pulse doesn't go off, but the bird manages to get it caught on a limb and strangles itself with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hornets from Hell (that is the actual title of this documentary)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oh god why did you make me go to google

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, I've corrected these errors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This aviary is to contain plants and insect life typical of an Iraqi forest for the purpose of SCP-1436’s nutrition.

What kind of forest? Why is Iraq suddenly a biome?

SCP-1436 is a male bird of unidentified species with features similar to those of the Meropidae family. However, SCP-1436 is larger than any known Meropidae species with a wingspan of one meter.

Proportionally that bee you have in the pic is impossibru.

SCP-1436’s behavior is typical of an insect-eating bird and it produces sounds that have been identified as attempts to communicate with other members of its own species.

The word you are looking for is insectivore.
"produces sounds"—-> calls

and SCP-1436 renters the growth stage

You mean reenters not "renters". Renters implies that this thing is renting out its time. Somehow…

The last cycle before SCP-1436’s recovery by the foundation resulted in the

Foundation.

and continues to takes act for self-preservation during the second stage.

"continues to take acts of self-preservation"

Various Foundation attempts to kill SCP-1436 at the beginning of the second stage have failed, the most recent of which resulted in the firearm intended to shoot SCP-1436 misfiring and injuring the researcher carrying out the test, as well as rendering the firearm permanently inoperable.

This tone…is bad.

Cut "various".

"Testing during this phase has thus-far failed to kill SCP-1436. The most recent ended in the misfire of a [insert firearm here], injuring the researcher and rendering the [insert firearm here] inoperable."

Once the second stage events have succeeded in killing SCP-1436 it typically fully decomposes, including bone, in 20 hours

Okay…wait.

So the cycle causes improbable events to kill the bird but prevents other events from killing it? Given that this thing can cause "complex causal chains" to facilitate stage 2 why is any event prevented at all? The Foundation could literally be pulled into the causal chain and kill the thing with no problems.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept, "probability phoenix", is sound. The execution here is lackluster. My suggestions (which others have already offered) are:
- Make it a normal-sized bird. The concept is distracting and the photo makes it confusing.
- Needs a partial event log to drive home the presentation of the concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea behind this is OK, though it seems to be 1007 as a bird. While definitely not identical, I think the two are just too close thematically.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, I can't do any work on this today (going to be really busy), but tomorrow I'll try to add an addendum of some sort, as that's what seems to be missing. Photosynthetic if you'd be willing to look it over from a biological standpoint that would be great. Thanks for your feedback everyone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the SCP, it's well written and tone is right I'm giving a +1 to it for that.

But please, expand it a bit more, I think it could have some "improvable ways to die" written as incident logs there, like "The bird ate a bug, the bug was too big to swallow it, the bird died because it couldn't breath." Or "The bird flies up to a researcher arm, the researcher accidentaly falls, the brid flies around scared, the researcher falls on the bird, crushing it."

You know you can make lots o' ways to die with this bird, including humorous ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept has potential, but the execution's kind of lackluster. Others have covered most of my points in that respect; I would add only that the science here made me a little squinty. Get a couple of our experts in biology and math or physics to look it over, please (I volunteer for the biology, if you like).

e: dammit keyboard

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The SCP is also allergic to bees. Just another way of getting itself killed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: there needs to be an article for the bee

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If that picture is of a the SCP, and the SCP has a 1 meter wingspan, that bee is fucking massive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The containment procedures are what I like best

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fact that it's method of death is statistically improbable events, it'd probably be really difficult to make it non-humorous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, I still won't upvote this, and I might come back and downvote it again once the novelty wears off, but upon rereading this, I just found the line "SCP-1436 is never to be contained at any location with on-site warheads" and it amused the hell out of me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I second this, but I don't think it has to be a humorous log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, it's like a probability phoenix instead of a fire one. I will say that until I hit "it causes weird shit to hasten its own death", it wasn't all that interesting, so it could use some work still. (Falling Objects' suggestion above is not a bad one.) Upvote to keep it above water.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this article could be much better with a lightly humorous log containing a number of events, each extremely improbable. Something like SCP-1436 being crushed by falling luggage, and the Foundation determining that a routine passenger airliner's cargo hold experienced a malfunction and their loading bay was open.

Or something like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had to read this over again until I understood it, but I suppose the thing that is most intriguing is that the bird can be the cause of some pretty strange accidents before it finally dies. I think that would be a fun thing to expand on, like detailing how it effected the Foundation after recovery. I mean how many insane things can you imagine would happen until they finally realized what to do with it? The containment procedures seem like they were put in place because they knew exactly what was going on immediately upon recovery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, one of my pet peeves is the misuse of entropy. Just because something goes on forever does not mean it has anything to do with entropy. In fact, this is quite clearly shown to have positive entropy during most stages, and a static entropy overall (which is not anomalous, as the phoenix is not a truly isolated system).
tl:dr- Don't use entropy unless you understand it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ouch. But I suppose you're right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I was worried that I didn't have enough of a hook.

There's a hook present here? Wanna let us in on it?

Edit: I apologize for sounding rude. I just felt like nothing was done with this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I was worried that I didn't have enough of a hook. Thank you all for you feedback, I will think about how to incorporate it. Maybe an incident log?
I'm going to leave it up to get some more feedback, as the chat seemed to like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well written, but there's no story here. Neutral vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't particularly like this one. It just seems to be a bird that gets reborn with no real story to tell. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not badly written, but fairly boring. I'd have liked to see some more detailed examples of the crazy shit this bird does to make itself die, and maybe something as to why its been made this way. There's definitely promise in this idea, but overall I failed to find it interesting as it currently stands. Run this by the chat next time, or the forums if you haven't already. Your tone was pretty good, though, so I think that with the right idea, you could write something that'd stick.
